questions.question: Explain the four necessary conditions for deadlock with brief examples.
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion: Only one process can use resource at a time (e.g., printer)
Hold and Wait: Process holds resources while waiting for others (e.g., holding file while waiting for printer)
No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken away (e.g., cannot steal lock)
Circular Wait: Circular chain of processes each waiting for next (P1→P2→P1)

questions.question: What is a Wait-For Graph? How is it used in distributed deadlock detection?
keyPoints: Definition: Directed graph with nodes=processes, edges=wait-for relationship
Edge P1→P2 means P1 waiting for resource held by P2
Cycle in WFG indicates potential deadlock
Each site maintains local WFG; exchanged to detect global cycles

questions.question: Describe the Chandy-Misra-Haas algorithm for distributed deadlock detection.
keyPoints: Uses edge chasing technique with probe messages
Probe contains (initiator, sender, blocker) fields
Initiator sends probes along wait-for edges
Deadlock confirmed when probe returns to initiator
Process then initiates resolution

questions.question: What are phantom (false) deadlocks in distributed systems? Explain causes.
keyPoints: Phantom deadlock: Detected but doesn't actually exist
Causes: Asynchronous message delivery timing
Stale information in wait-for graphs
Message delays during detection
Can cause unnecessary process termination

questions.question: Differentiate between AND and OR models of wait-for in distributed systems.
keyPoints: AND model: Process waits for ALL requested resources simultaneously
OR model: Process waits for ANY ONE resource to become available
AND: Acquires all or none; OR: Acquires first available then continues
Deadlock detection differs for each model

questions.question: Explain three control organizations for distributed deadlock detection.
keyPoints: Centralized: One coordinator site; simple but single point of failure
Hierarchical: Tree of coordinators; reduces bottleneck
Distributed: All sites participate equally; complex but robust

questions.question: Compare deadlock prevention, avoidance, and detection approaches.
keyPoints: Prevention: Ensure one Coffman condition cannot hold (e.g., ordered resources)
Avoidance: Use algorithms (Banker's) to safely allocate using future needs
Detection: Allow deadlocks, periodically detect, then resolve
Trade-off: Prevention most restrictive, detection most flexible

questions.question: Explain the Chandy-Misra-Haas distributed deadlock detection algorithm in detail with an example. How does it handle false deadlocks?
keyPoints: Algorithm uses edge chasing with probe messages
Probe format: (initiator_id, sender_id, blocker_id)
Process initiates probe when blocked and has no outgoing edges
Probe traverses wait-for edges
Deadlock when probe returns to initiator (cycle detected)
Resolution: Initiator sends resolve message, processes abort
False deadlock handling: Use timestamps, accept some false positives
Optimization: Each process involved only once in probe path

questions.question: Discuss the different approaches for handling deadlocks in distributed systems. Explain why distributed deadlock detection is more challenging than centralized.
keyPoints: Approaches: Prevention (break conditions), Avoidance (Banker's algorithm), Detection (find and resolve)
Distributed challenges: No global clock, no shared memory
Each site has incomplete local information
Message delays cause stale WFG
Phantom deadlocks due to timing
Control overhead for coordination
Various algorithms: CMH (edge chasing), path pushing, hierarchical approaches

questions.question: Design a centralized deadlock detection scheme for a distributed system. What are its advantages and disadvantages compared to distributed detection?
keyPoints: Centralized: One coordinator site receives wait-for info from all sites
Each site sends local WFG to coordinator periodically or on change
Coordinator builds global WFG and checks for cycles
Advantages: Simple to implement, accurate global view, easy cycle detection
Disadvantages: Single point of failure, bottleneck, message overhead
Scalability issues with large systems
Synchronization needed for WFG updates

questions.question: Explain the concept of resource allocation graph and wait-for graph. How are they used in deadlock detection? Show with a diagram example.
keyPoints: RAG: Shows resource instances allocated to processes, processes requesting resources
WFG: Simplified view showing only wait-for relationships between processes
RAG: Rectangle=resource, circle=process, edge from process to resource=requested, edge from resource to process=allocated
WFG: Direct edge from waiting process to holder process
Cycle in RAG or WFG indicates potential deadlock
RAG accurate for single-instance resources
WFG more common in distributed systems
